subtopics.subtopic_description: Educational background or training
notes: Holds a Bachelor’s Degree in Computer Engineering from a recognized university
Currently pursuing a PhD in Computer Engineering at Northeastern University
Received foundational training in mathematics, physics, and electrical engineering principles during undergraduate studies
Regularly attends workshops and seminars related to AR/VR and communication technologies as part of PhD training

subtopics.subtopic_description: Specific job title and role description
notes: PhD Student in Computer Engineering at Northeastern University
Researcher with the Spatial Internet Research Group (SINRG), led by Professor Mallesham Dasari
Focuses on AR/VR systems, immersive media, and next-generation communications
Primary responsibilities include research, systems development, academic writing, and presentations

subtopics.subtopic_description: Current industry or sector (e.g., tech, finance, manufacturing)
notes: Academic research sector with a focus on emerging technologies
Active in the technology and engineering sector, specifically computer engineering and immersive media
Works at the intersection of academia and tech innovation, often collaborating with industry partners
Involved in the broader AR/VR and AI research communities

subtopics.subtopic_description: Company size and environment
notes: Affiliated with Northeastern University, a large research-focused academic institution
Works within a small, collaborative research group (SINRG) of approximately 10-15 members
Regularly interacts with faculty, postdocs, and graduate students in a team-oriented environment
Environment encourages open-source contributions and sharing of research outcomes

subtopics.subtopic_description: Type of business or market segment
notes: Academic research and higher education
Specializes in AR/VR, immersive media, and spatial computing research
Engages in technology transfer and open-source software development for the academic and tech communities
Contributes to both fundamental research and applied solutions for next-generation communication

subtopics.subtopic_description: Duration/years of experience in current role
notes: Has 1-2 years of experience as a PhD student and researcher
Began current role shortly after completing undergraduate studies
Previously gained research and project experience during undergraduate education

subtopics.subtopic_description: Professional seniority or career level
notes: Early-career researcher and graduate student
Not in a managerial or supervisory position, but may mentor undergraduate assistants
Operates under the supervision of a principal investigator (Professor Dasari)
Participates in team discussions and collaborative decision-making

subtopics.subtopic_description: Primary job responsibilities and regular daily tasks
notes: Codes and develops systems and algorithms for AR/VR and 3D content compression research projects
Writes academic papers, technical documentation, and open-source project readmes
Reads and synthesizes recent literature to inform and inspire research directions
Prepares slides and presentations for conferences, group meetings, and academic seminars
Collaborates with peers for experiments, evaluations, and troubleshooting

subtopics.subtopic_description: Approximate proportion of time spent on core activities
notes: Spends approximately 40% of time coding and system development
Dedicates about 25% of time to academic writing and paper preparation
Allocates 15% of time to reading and summarizing literature
Spends 10% of time on open-sourcing work and maintaining repositories
Remainder of time is split between preparing presentations and attending meetings

subtopics.subtopic_description: Level of autonomy and scope of decision-making in the role
notes: Has autonomy in designing experiments and selecting technical approaches for assigned research problems
Major research directions and project choices are guided by the principal investigator
Makes independent decisions on coding strategies, tool selection, and daily workflow
Collaborates in group meetings to discuss and decide on research priorities

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks that feel easiest or most natural to perform
notes: Coding and implementing algorithms is typically the most natural task due to strong programming background
Summarizing research papers comes easily after regular practice during undergraduate and graduate studies
Using version control systems like Git is highly familiar and routine
Preparing visualizations and figures for presentations is straightforward with established toolsets

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks perceived as most challenging or complex
notes: Re-implementing complex published work without available baselines or open-source code is particularly challenging
Writing academic papers can be daunting, especially when articulating novel contributions
Debugging intricate system-level errors in multimedia frameworks (e.g., FFmpeg, x.265) often requires deep troubleshooting
Balancing time between research, experiments, and documentation under tight deadlines can be difficult

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks that are repetitive, data-heavy, or suitable for automation
notes: Preprocessing and formatting large 3D datasets for training or compression are highly repetitive
Compiling literature reviews and extracting key insights from papers could benefit from automation
Generating boilerplate code and documentation for open-source releases is routine and suitable for AI assistance
Converting experiment results into standardized plots and tables can be automated

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks that are most enjoyable or engaging versus those that feel boring or tedious
notes: Designing novel algorithms and conducting new experiments is highly engaging
Collaborating with peers on creative problem-solving is enjoyable
Repetitive data cleaning, formatting, or bug-fixing is tedious and less satisfying
Formatting papers to meet strict conference templates can feel monotonous

subtopics.subtopic_description: Common pain points or inefficiencies in completing tasks
notes: Lack of open-source baselines makes re-implementation time-consuming
Switching between multiple software tools can introduce workflow friction
Manually formatting documents in LaTeX is error-prone and time-consuming
Troubleshooting tool compatibility issues (e.g., FFmpeg with Python bindings) can slow progress

subtopics.subtopic_description: How enjoyment, skill level, and productivity relate to one another
notes: Higher enjoyment is typically linked to tasks where skill and autonomy are high, such as coding new algorithms
Productivity peaks when working on engaging projects with clear milestones
Tedious or low-skill tasks, like formatting or repetitive data handling, can reduce motivation and slow output
Learning new skills (e.g., advanced AI methods) can be both enjoyable and increase long-term productivity

subtopics.subtopic_description: Attitude towards learning new technologies and tools
notes: Enthusiastic about exploring and adopting new technologies that can boost research efficiency
Sees rapid tech evolution as an opportunity for skill growth
Willing to experiment with emerging tools, especially those relevant to AR/VR and AI
Occasionally cautious about investing time in tools with steep learning curves unless highly relevant

subtopics.subtopic_description: Perceived adaptability to new software/methods
notes: Adapts quickly to new coding environments, IDEs, and open-source libraries
Has rapidly integrated LLM-based tools (e.g., ChatGPT, Cursor) into daily workflow
Comfortable switching between different programming languages as required by research projects
Occasionally experiences friction when tools lack good documentation

subtopics.subtopic_description: Willingness to invest time in tech training
notes: Regularly allocates time to learn about new AI and AR/VR frameworks
Participates in online courses or webinars when new tools are directly relevant
Prefers self-directed, just-in-time learning using online resources or peer advice
Balances training time with research deadlines and publication goals

subtopics.subtopic_description: Motivations or barriers to learning new tech (e.g., workload, relevance)
notes: Motivated by potential to streamline research and solve previously intractable problems
High workload or tight deadlines can be a barrier to exploring non-critical new tools
Relevance to current research topics is a key factor in prioritizing what to learn
Peer recommendations can prompt a trial of new technologies

subtopics.subtopic_description: Influence of peers or management on willingness to adopt new tools
notes: Peers in the research group often share tips and recommend effective tools
Professor and research advisors encourage experimentation with new technologies
Adoption is accelerated when team members demonstrate tangible benefits
Collaborative environment fosters sharing of scripts, plugins, and workflow improvements

topic: Primary Tools and Technologies Used in Work

subtopics.subtopic_description: Specific software, platforms, or systems used daily
notes: Uses VS Code for code editing and debugging on a daily basis
Relies on Anaconda and PyCharm for Python development and package management
Employs Visual Studio for C++ and system-level development
Utilizes Git for version control and collaborative code management
Interacts with LLMs (e.g., ChatGPT, Cursor) each day for coding and writing support

subtopics.subtopic_description: Essential non-AI tools for workflow
notes: Uses LaTeX for academic paper writing and technical documentation
Employs FFmpeg and x.265 for video processing and compression tasks
Relies on Zotero for literature management and citation organization
Uses shell scripting for automating experiment pipelines

subtopics.subtopic_description: Familiarity with industry-standard technologies
notes: Proficient in Python and C++ for system and algorithm development
Familiar with standard multimedia libraries and codecs used in AR/VR and video compression
Regular user of Git and GitHub for code sharing and collaboration
Comfortable with cross-platform development environments (Windows, Linux, macOS)

subtopics.subtopic_description: Interoperability or integration issues between tools
notes: Occasional compatibility issues between Python packages and system-level tools like FFmpeg
Challenges integrating LaTeX outputs with graphical assets from other tools
Version conflicts can arise between libraries managed by Anaconda and native system packages
Uses workarounds such as containerization or virtual environments to improve tool interoperability

subtopics.subtopic_description: Familiarity with fundamental AI/ML concepts and terminology
notes: Comfortable with concepts such as supervised/unsupervised learning, neural networks, and model evaluation metrics
Regularly reads AI/ML papers to stay current with field developments
Understands practical applications of generative AI (e.g., LLMs) in research workflows
Familiar with AI-driven compression and video streaming methods relevant to current research

subtopics.subtopic_description: Names of specific AI software/platforms currently used in work
notes: Uses ChatGPT and Grok for code assistance, literature review, and writing support
Employs Cursor for AI-powered code completion and debugging
Occasionally experiments with open-source AI toolkits such as Hugging Face Transformers
Has hands-on experience with AI-based video compression libraries

subtopics.subtopic_description: Frequency and purpose of AI tool application (specific use cases)
notes: Uses LLMs daily to troubleshoot coding problems and accelerate development
Relies on AI assistants for summarizing research papers and extracting insights
Employs AI-generated suggestions to improve academic writing and clarity
Leverages AI for automating repetitive data processing and code documentation

subtopics.subtopic_description: Specific examples of AI success and failure experiences (lessons learned)
notes: Successfully used ChatGPT to resolve a challenging bug in a video streaming pipeline within hours
AI-generated code snippets have occasionally required significant debugging and human correction
Effective at using LLMs to generate first drafts of literature reviews, saving substantial time
Learned to always manually verify AI outputs, especially for technical accuracy in publications

subtopics.subtopic_description: Availability of organizational training or peer resources for AI use
notes: No formal AI tool training provided by the university or research group
Peers frequently share tips and best practices for LLM use in Slack or group meetings
Self-directed learning is the primary mode of AI tool adoption
Occasional workshops or invited talks on AI topics are available at Northeastern University

topic: AI Interaction Style and Workflow Change

subtopics.subtopic_description: Preferred mode of interaction: independent versus step-by-step collaboration
notes: Prefers step-by-step collaboration with AI tools, iteratively refining prompts and outputs
Often starts with a broad query and narrows down based on AI responses
Combines independent critical thinking with guided assistance from LLMs
Uses AI both for brainstorming ideas and for detailed, task-specific support

subtopics.subtopic_description: Style of human-AI teaming (e.g., advisor, assistant, co-worker)
notes: Views AI as an intelligent assistant that augments, but does not replace, human expertise
Treats AI as a brainstorming partner for coding and writing tasks
Relies on AI for rapid prototyping and initial drafts, then brings in human judgment and refinement
Occasionally treats AI as an advisor for unfamiliar technical areas

subtopics.subtopic_description: Willingness and openness to adopting new AI-driven workflows
notes: Highly open to experimenting with new AI-powered tools that promise efficiency gains
Quick to integrate LLMs into coding, writing, and literature review processes
Prefers to pilot new workflows on low-stakes tasks before full adoption
Encourages peers to share feedback on novel AI-driven approaches

subtopics.subtopic_description: Preference for conversational vs. command-based interfaces (communication dynamics)
notes: Prefers conversational interfaces for brainstorming, problem-solving, and quick Q&A
Uses command-based interfaces for scripting or batch processing tasks where precision is needed
Finds conversational AI more intuitive for complex, ambiguous queries
Switches between interface types depending on context and task complexity

subtopics.subtopic_description: Extent to which tasks rely on specialized, tacit domain knowledge
notes: Many research tasks require deep understanding of AR/VR systems and 3D media compression
Domain-specific decisions, such as algorithm selection, rely on expertise not always captured in public datasets
Tacit knowledge includes interpreting ambiguous experimental results and identifying novel contributions
AI can assist with routine or well-defined tasks, but human insight is critical for research innovation

subtopics.subtopic_description: Level of trust in AI outputs for work tasks and critical decisions
notes: Trusts AI-generated code and summaries as a starting point, but always reviews and validates outputs
Generally cautious about using AI suggestions directly in published work or experiments
High-stakes decisions, such as experimental design or paper submission, are made by humans
More trust is placed in AI for well-defined, rule-based tasks than for open-ended research

subtopics.subtopic_description: Ideal balance of human effort and AI automation for specific tasks
notes: Prefers AI to handle repetitive, data-intensive, or formatting-heavy tasks
Human oversight is essential for creative, strategic, or interpretive aspects of research
Sees AI as a force multiplier that frees up time for higher-level thinking and innovation
Aims for a collaborative workflow where AI augments, but does not fully automate, research

subtopics.subtopic_description: Conditions under which high automation is acceptable or threatening
notes: Accepts high automation for tasks with clear, objective outcomes (e.g., data preprocessing, code linting)
Finds automation threatening if it could obscure critical errors or reduce skill development
Prefers manual review of AI-driven outputs in high-impact scenarios (e.g., paper writing, experiment results)
Supports automation in areas where human error is common or tasks are highly repetitive

topic: AI Impact on Skills and Job Security

subtopics.subtopic_description: Perceived impact of AI on the importance of existing skills (enhanced vs. reduced)
notes: Believes AI enhances productivity by automating low-level tasks, allowing greater focus on creative problem-solving
Research and critical thinking skills remain essential, even as some technical tasks are automated
Programming fundamentals are still required to effectively use and validate AI-generated code
Routine skills (e.g., manual formatting, basic literature review) are less emphasized due to AI support

subtopics.subtopic_description: Emerging skills or new areas of responsibility created by AI
notes: Developing prompt engineering skills to maximize the utility of LLMs
Learning to critically assess and validate AI-generated outputs
Gaining familiarity with integrating AI APIs and tools into existing workflows
Taking on new responsibilities in AI tool evaluation and peer training

subtopics.subtopic_description: Level of concern about AI replacing specific tasks or the overall role
notes: Low concern about AI replacing core research or creative tasks
Some routine responsibilities (e.g., data cleaning, code documentation) may be fully automated
Sees AI as a tool that shifts the role toward higher-level analysis and innovation
Acknowledges the need to stay current with AI advancements to remain competitive

subtopics.subtopic_description: Availability of override mechanisms or manual checks for AI-driven processes
notes: Always reviews AI-generated code and text before integrating into research outputs
Manual checks are standard for experimental results and publication submissions
Peer review within the research group acts as an additional safeguard
Prefers AI tools that provide transparency and allow easy rollback to manual control

subtopics.subtopic_description: Perceived change in team or company policies regarding AI adoption
notes: Research group has become more open to AI tool adoption in the past year
Informal best practices and guidelines for AI tool use are shared among team members
No formal, organization-wide AI usage policy yet in place
Encouraged to experiment and report back on AI tool effectiveness and limitations

subtopics.subtopic_description: General outlook on AI's broader societal and industry impact
notes: Sees AI as transformative for research productivity and scientific discovery
Believes AI will democratize access to complex tools and lower barriers to entry in tech fields
Expects AI to drive major advances in media, communication, and immersive technology
Recognizes potential for AI to disrupt traditional job roles, requiring continuous learning

subtopics.subtopic_description: Personal beliefs about the ethics and risks of AI in the workplace
notes: Believes transparency and explainability are critical for responsible AI use in research
Cautious about relying on AI for tasks where bias or error could have significant consequences
Supports development of ethical guidelines for AI in academic publishing
Values human oversight to prevent overreliance on potentially flawed AI outputs

subtopics.subtopic_description: Missing AI tools or features that would be most beneficial in the future
notes: Wishes for AI that can fully and faithfully re-implement any published work for benchmarking
Desires automated slide, presentation, and poster generation tailored to academic standards
Would benefit from AI tools that deeply understand and summarize highly technical literature
Needs smarter AI assistants for experiment orchestration and result analysis

subtopics.subtopic_description: Predicted evolution of their job in the next 5-10 years with AI integration
notes: Anticipates increased reliance on AI for routine research tasks and documentation
Expects to spend more time on conceptual work and cross-disciplinary innovation
Believes roles will shift toward AI tool development, integration, and oversight
Predicts greater collaboration between researchers and intelligent AI systems

subtopics.subtopic_description: Concrete steps they would want their organization to take regarding AI strategy
notes: Develop clear guidelines for AI tool use in research and publication
Offer formal training and workshops on advanced AI tools and best practices
Encourage open-source and transparent AI research within the academic community
Foster peer collaboration to share AI tool experiences and workflow optimizations
